title: Amazon Prime Cancels "Babies Are Still Murdered Here"
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States reports that Amazon Prime removed the abolitionist documentary 'Babies Are Still Murdered Here' on July 20, 2020, citing customer feedback that the film's strong viewer ratings appear to contradict. The article also recaps the film's content, which critiques mainstream pro-life leaders and tells the story of Jeremiah Thomas's appeal to Texas Governor Greg Abbott.

questions.question: When did Amazon Prime remove 'Babies Are Still Murdered Here' and how long had it been available?
questions.answer: The documentary was removed on July 20, 2020, after being available to Amazon Prime users since November 2019.
questions.quote: The anti-abortion documentary *Babies Are Still Murdered Here*, which had been available to Amazon Prime users since November of 2019*,* was removed from the streaming service on July 20.

questions.question: What reason did Amazon give for pulling the documentary?
questions.answer: Amazon attributed the removal to customer feedback, claiming the title did not meet its content quality expectations based on a combination of star reviews, customer reviews, and viewing behavior.
questions.quote: Customer feedback (included but not limited to a combination of star reviews, customer reviews, and/or customer viewing behavior) has indicated that this title does not meet our customer content quality expectations.

questions.question: How do the actual viewer ratings stack up against Amazon's stated rationale?
questions.answer: The reviews directly contradict Amazon's explanation: 89 percent of 344 reviewers gave the film five stars, with an overall average of 4.7 stars.
questions.quote: The film [received](https://www.amazon.com/product-reviews/B081MQ72DB/ref=cm_cr_unknown?ie=UTF8&filterByStar=one_star&reviewerType=all_reviews&pageNumber=1#reviews-filter-bar) five stars from 89 percent of the 344 reviewers as of the publishing of this article, with an average of 4.7 stars among all reviews.

questions.question: How did director Marcus Pittman respond to Amazon's decision in light of recent Big Tech testimony?
questions.answer: Pittman pointed to congressional testimony in which Bezos and other tech executives claimed political neutrality, then argued that the strong ratings and Amazon's own keywords proved viewers liked the film, making the removal indefensible.
questions.quote: The big tech executives were just meeting before Congress this week talking about all this sort of stuff. 'Oh, we're trying to be fair and balanced and we just want to get rid of misinformation.' Well, look, the people that were watching this movie were liking this movie. There's evidence of that. The screenshots [of the ratings] show that people like it. The keywords that Amazon itself picked out show that people liked it. And yet here we are. It was removed. It was removed. And it was getting quite a bit of views. So the fact that Amazon Prime did this is insane to me.

questions.question: What is the core argument of 'Babies Are Still Murdered Here'?
questions.answer: The film critiques pro-life lobbyists and politicians for opposing legislation that would abolish abortion and for arguments that lack Biblical foundations.
questions.quote: The documentary focuses on the opposition of pro-life lobbyists and politicians to legislation that abolishes abortion and the lack of Biblical foundations in the arguments and strategies used by pro-life leaders.

questions.question: Which pro-life leaders are featured in interviews showing opposition to abolitionism?
questions.answer: The film features National Right to Life Committee President Carol Tobias and Virginia Society for Human Life President Olivia Gans Turner expressing opposition to Biblical foundations and to criminalizing abortion.
questions.quote: Early portions of the film feature interviews with National Right to Life Committee (NRLC) President Carol Tobias and Virginia Society for Human Life President Olivia Gans Turner in which they express their opposition to Biblical foundations and to the criminalization of abortion.

questions.question: What was Jeremiah Thomas's role in the film and in Texas abolition efforts?
questions.answer: Thomas, a 16-year-old dying of bone cancer, used his Make-a-Wish in June 2018 to call Governor Greg Abbott and ask him to abolish abortion in Texas; Abbott responded positively about pursuing it in the next session.
questions.quote: On June 18, 2018, the 16-year-old Thomas, who was in his hospital bed with bone cancer which would take his life a few months later, used his Make-a-Wish to obtain a phone call with Texas Governor Greg Abbott. When Thomas asked Abbott to use his influence to abolish abortion in Texas, Abbott responded positively noting that abortion's abolition was going to be pursued in the upcoming legislative session.

questions.question: Why didn't The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act move forward after Thomas's appeal?
questions.answer: Pro-life Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach denied the bill a committee vote, ensuring abortion remained legal in the state for another two years.
questions.quote: As documented in the film, Texas State Rep. Jeff Leach, who is pro-life, denied The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act a committee vote and thereby ensured that abortion would remain legal in the state for another two years.

questions.question: Where can the documentary still be watched after Amazon's removal?
questions.answer: The film remains available in full on the Facebook and YouTube pages of Apologia Studios.
questions.quote: While it is no longer available for purchase or rent on Amazon Prime, *Babies Are Still Murdered Here* can still be watched in its entirety on the [Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/watch/live/?v=417279289168895&ref=search) and [YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?time_continue=5248&v=9-vyYE6C8MU&feature=emb_logo) pages of Apologia Studios.

related.title: Jeff Leach Delivers Death Sentence
related.reason: Directly covers the same Texas legislator the film blames for killing The Abolition of Abortion in Texas Act.

related.reason: Follow-up reporting on the same Texas abolition bill featured in the documentary.

related.title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
related.reason: Continues the legislative story of attempts to abolish abortion in Texas after the events shown in the film.

related.title: Big Abolitionist Candidate Advances to Runoff Against Chair Who Kept Abortion Legal in Texas
related.reason: Electoral fallout against the Texas committee chair who blocked abolition, the same political dynamic critiqued in the documentary.

related.title: Peeling Back the Texas Pro-Life Facade
related.reason: Expands on the documentary's central thesis that mainstream Texas pro-life leaders obstruct abolition.

related.title: National Right to Life Endorses Greg McCortney
related.reason: Provides further critique of NRLC, the organization whose president is interviewed opposing abolition in the film.

related.title: Contra Klusendorf and Desiring God: Pro-Life Compromise Dooms Preborn Children
related.reason: Echoes the documentary's argument that pro-life compromise lacks Biblical foundation and prolongs abortion.
